entries.title: What business logic errors are
entries.body:
Business logic flaws are vulnerabilities that arise not from code defects (like SQLi or XSS) but from the application's business rules themselves: pricing models, transaction limits, the sequence of steps in a multi-stage process.

The key difference: you do not break the code with unfiltered input; instead you use the regular, intended functionality in a way the developer did not anticipate, and you produce outcomes undesirable for the business. That is why such bugs are almost never caught by scanners and require domain knowledge.

entries.title: Where to look for logic bugs
entries.body:
Look where there is money, privileges, and multi-step processes:
• Cart, checkout, payment, refunds, subscriptions, premium features.
• Coupons/promo codes, bonuses, referral credits, cashback.
• Any fields with price, quantity, currency, delivery fee.
• Multi-step workflows (registration → KYC → payment, a wizard of several pages).
• Reviews, ratings, comments, votes - anywhere there is a "once only" limit.

Approach: walk through the legitimate scenario end to end, recording the traffic in Burp, and at each step ask "what happens if I do this out of order / repeat it / send an unexpected value / do it in parallel?".

entries.title: Negative quantities and values in the cart
entries.body:
• Add items with a negative quantity together with other items to balance out (reduce) the total.
• Try negative values for the delivery fee - sometimes this reduces the total amount due.
• Check rounding: unit price × negative/fractional quantity may yield a favorable total.

Where to edit: quantity, unit price, discount/fee fields in the request body - server-side validation is often missing.

entries.title: Exceeding stock and limits
entries.body:
• Try adding more of an item to the cart than is in stock.
• Check for overflow: a very large quantity may overflow the integer type and wrap into a negative/zero total.
• Check "no more than N per account/order" limits - they are bypassed by repeating the request, splitting into multiple line items, or a race condition.

entries.title: Rounding error
entries.body:
A classic example is a bug in the internal transfer system of a crypto platform (XBT/Bitcoin), where rounding lets you create money out of thin air.

The attacker initiates a transfer of 0.000000005 XBT (0.5 satoshi) - which is below the system's minimum precision (1 satoshi):
• The sender's balance does not change: the algorithm rounds the debited amount down to 0 satoshi.
• The recipient's balance grows by 1 satoshi (0.00000001): the algorithm rounds the credited amount up to 1 satoshi.

Result: 0.00000001 XBT generated out of nothing. With no rate-limit, OTP, or anti-fraud, the process is automated and repeated endlessly, effectively printing money. Root cause of the bug: instead of rejecting the transfer / enforcing a minimum, the system ignores the debit from the sender but credits the recipient. (Source: hackerone #176461.)

entries.title: Delivery-fee manipulation
entries.body:
• Substitute negative values for the delivery cost - this may reduce the total.
• Check whether free delivery can be enabled by changing request parameters (a free_delivery flag, zeroing out the shipping field).

entries.title: Currency arbitrage
entries.body:
• Try paying in one currency (for example, USD) and requesting the refund in another (for example, EUR). The difference in conversion rates may yield a profit.
• Look for the same trick in any refund and balance-exchange scenarios between currencies.

entries.title: Premium features without a subscription
entries.body:
• Check access to premium-only sections/endpoints without an active subscription (direct navigation to the URL, direct API call).
• Buy a premium feature, cancel it, and check whether it remains accessible after the refund.
• Look for true/false values in requests/responses that validate premium access; change them via Burp Match & Replace to gain unauthorized access.
• Inspect cookies and localStorage for variables confirming premium access (isPremium, plan=pro).

entries.title: Refund abuse
entries.body:
• Buy an item, request a refund, and check whether the item/content stays accessible after the refund.
• Look for currency-arbitrage opportunities on refunds.
• Send several subscription-cancellation requests to check whether you can get refunded multiple times for a single purchase.

entries.title: Trusting client-side data
entries.body:
The basic class of logic bugs - the server trusts data that the client fully controls.

• Look in the traffic for fields that determine price, discount, privileges, payment status, role, limits.
• Inspect cookies, localStorage, hidden form fields, and request parameters for access flags (isAdmin, isPremium, paid=true).
• Use Burp Match & Replace to automatically substitute such values with the ones you want and see whether the server re-validates them on its side.
• If the final price/amount is sent by the client, it is almost always a bug: the price must be computed only on the server.

entries.title: Mass Assignment and HTTP Parameter Pollution
entries.body:
• Check for Mass Assignment: add fields to the request body that are not in the form (role, balance, discount, is_verified) and see whether the backend accepts them.
• Check for HTTP Parameter Pollution: duplicate a parameter (for example, code=A&code=B) to apply multiple values where the application expects one.
• A typical case is applying multiple promo codes at once, even though the interface allows only one.

entries.title: Testing promo codes and discounts
entries.body:
• Apply the same promo code several times - check whether it is reusable.
• If the code is single-use/unique, check for a race condition: apply one code on two accounts simultaneously.
• Mass Assignment / HPP - try applying several discount codes when the application expects one.
• Check the code input field for missing sanitization: XSS, SQL Injection.
• Try applying a discount to non-discounted items by manipulating the server request (substituting the item/category id).

entries.title: Testing the review feature
entries.body:
• Check whether you can leave a review as a "verified buyer" without having bought the item.
• Give a rating outside the allowed scale: 0, 6, or a negative number in a 1-5 system.
• Check whether a single user can leave multiple ratings on one item (useful for detecting races).
• Check whether the file-upload field accepts any extensions - protection on such endpoints is often forgotten.
• Try leaving a review on behalf of another user (impersonation).
• Check for CSRF on this feature - it is often not protected by tokens.

entries.title: Testing comments in a thread
entries.body:
• Check whether there is a limit on the number of comments in a thread.
• If a user can comment only once, use a race condition to check whether you can post multiple comments.
• If the system allows comments only from verified/privileged users, supply those parameters and check whether you can comment.
• Try posting comments on behalf of another user (impersonation).

entries.title: Race conditions in business logic
entries.body:
A race condition breaks "only once / no more than N" limit checks that the application performs sequentially (check → then write). If you send several requests simultaneously, they all pass the check before the state is updated.

Where to apply:
• Single-use/unique promo code - apply in parallel across several accounts or multiple times on one.
• "One review/rating/comment per user" - post several at once.
• Multiple refunds/cancellations for a single purchase.
• Debiting balance/bonuses twice, withdrawing funds beyond the remaining balance.

How to test: assemble a reference request and send a batch of identical parallel requests (Burp Repeater group → Send in parallel / single-packet attack, Turbo Intruder). A sign of the bug is success more than once or a total beyond the limit.

entries.title: Logic-bug testing checklist
entries.body:
Price and quantity
☐ Negative item quantity (balancing the total)
☐ Negative/zero price, discount, delivery fee
☐ Quantity overflow (huge number → negative total)
☐ Fractional values and rounding errors
☐ Exceeding stock on hand / per-order limit

Coupons and limits
☐ Re-applying a single promo code
☐ Multiple promo codes at once (Mass Assignment / HPP)
☐ Discount on a non-promo item (id substitution)
☐ Bypassing "once only" limits: reviews, ratings, comments

Workflow and access
☐ Access to premium endpoints without a subscription
☐ Access to a feature/content persists after a refund/cancellation
☐ Multiple refunds/cancellations for a single purchase
☐ Skipping/reordering steps of a multi-step process
☐ Currency arbitrage: pay in one currency, refund in another

Client trust and races
☐ Final price/amount/privileges come from the client and are not re-validated
☐ Substituting access flags in cookie/localStorage/hidden fields
☐ Races on "only once" checks (parallel requests)
☐ Impersonation: acting on behalf of another user
☐ CSRF on unprotected actions (reviews, etc.)
